mcqs.question: Which of the following is the formal definition of Big O notation?

options.text: f(n) = Ω(g(n)) if ∃c > 0 and n₀ ≥ 1 such that 0 ≤ f(n) ≤ c·g(n) for all n ≥ n₀
f(n) = O(g(n)) if ∃c > 0 and n₀ ≥ 1 such that 0 ≤ f(n) ≤ c·g(n) for all n ≥ n₀
f(n) = Θ(g(n)) if ∃c₁, c₂ > 0 and n₀ ≥ 1 such that c₁·g(n) ≤ f(n) ≤ c₂·g(n) for all n ≥ n₀
f(n) = o(g(n)) if lim_{n→∞} f(n)/g(n) = 0

mcqs.explanation: Big O notation provides an upper bound, meaning f(n) is bounded above by c·g(n) for sufficiently large n.

mcqs.question: Consider two algorithms with time complexities f(n) = n² + 3n and g(n) = 1000n². Which of the following relationships is correct?

options.text: f(n) = O(g(n))
f(n) = Ω(g(n))
f(n) = Θ(g(n))
f(n) = o(g(n))

mcqs.explanation: Since n² + 3n ≤ 1001n² for n ≥ 1, f(n) is O(g(n)) - the quadratic term dominates.

mcqs.question: The function f(n) = 5n³ + 2n² - n is Θ(n³). What can be said about the constants c₁ and c₂ in the formal definition?

options.text: c₁ must be less than 5 and c₂ must be greater than 5
c₁ must be 5 exactly and c₂ must be 5 exactly
c₁ can be any positive number and c₂ can be any positive number
c₁ must be greater than 5 and c₂ must be less than 5

mcqs.explanation: For Θ(n³), we need c₁·n³ ≤ 5n³ + 2n² - n ≤ c₂·n³, so c₁ < 5 and c₂ > 5 satisfy this for large n.

mcqs.question: Which property is NOT true for Big O notation?

options.text: Reflexivity: f(n) = O(f(n))
Transitivity: if f(n) = O(g(n)) and g(n) = O(h(n)), then f(n) = O(h(n))
Symmetry: f(n) = O(g(n)) implies g(n) = O(f(n))
If f(n) = O(g(n)) and g(n) = O(h(n)), then f(n) = O(h(n))

mcqs.explanation: Big O is not symmetric; it's a partial order relation. Symmetry only holds for Θ notation.

mcqs.question: Little omega notation ω(g(n)) means f(n) ∈ ω(g(n)) if:

options.text: ∃c > 0 such that f(n) ≥ c·g(n) for infinitely many n
lim_{n→∞} f(n)/g(n) = ∞
∃c > 0 and n₀ such that f(n) ≥ c·g(n) for all n ≥ n₀
lim_{n→∞} g(n)/f(n) = 0

mcqs.explanation: Little omega means f grows strictly faster than g, so the limit of f(n)/g(n) as n→∞ is infinity.

mcqs.question: An algorithm takes 100MB of memory for input size n, plus an additional 5n² bytes for auxiliary space. What is the space complexity in terms of n?

options.text: O(n)
O(n²)

mcqs.explanation: The auxiliary space 5n² dominates the constant 100MB when n grows, so space complexity is O(n²).

mcqs.question: Given f(n) = n! and g(n) = 2ⁿ, which statement is correct?

options.text: f(n) = O(g(n)) because factorial grows faster than exponential
f(n) = o(g(n)) because exponential grows faster than factorial
f(n) = ω(g(n)) because factorial grows faster than exponential
f(n) = Θ(g(n)) because both grow exponentially

mcqs.explanation: n! grows much faster than 2ⁿ for large n (Stirling's approximation shows n! ≈ (n/e)ⁿ), so f(n) = ω(g(n)).

mcqs.question: Which of the following is true about the relationship between little o and Big O notations?

options.text: If f(n) = o(g(n)), then f(n) = O(g(n)) always holds
If f(n) = O(g(n)), then f(n) = o(g(n)) always holds
o(g(n)) ⊂ O(g(n)) but O(g(n)) ⊄ o(g(n))
o(g(n)) and O(g(n)) are equivalent sets

mcqs.explanation: If f(n) = o(g(n)), it means f(n) grows strictly slower than g(n), which satisfies the looser upper bound condition of O(g(n)).
